options: By editing the deployed index.js file directly in the runtime console
By setting the new secret via the `aio` CLI or Developer Console and triggering a re-deployment to apply the new values
By modifying files inside the AEM dispatcher configuration
Secrets are static and cannot be rotated without creating a new application
questions.explanation: Rotating secrets involves updating the secure variables in the Adobe Developer Console or via `aio` CLI commands, which refreshes the container environment.

questions.question: How does App Builder handle static assets like CSS and HTML files for its custom admin UIs?
options: They are served directly from the database of AEM Author
They are deployed and hosted on Adobe's out-of-the-box CDN (surfaced via unique URL subdomains)
They must be hosted on external platforms like AWS S3 by the client
They are bundle-compiled and stored inside the database rows
questions.explanation: The CLI deploys custom administrative UIs to Adobe's built-in CDN storage, exposing them directly inside the Experience Cloud interface.

questions.question: Under what circumstance does App Builder support background, recurring scheduled workloads?
options: Actions can run indefinitely if marked as daemon processes
Through scheduled triggers (alarms) configured in the app manifest, executing stateless actions at set intervals
By running a persistent Node.js express server inside I/O Runtime
App Builder does not support cron-like tasks
questions.explanation: Manifest files can define cron-like alarm triggers that run designated actions on a schedule, though execution must remain within the 60-second limit.

questions.question: An architect is evaluating where to place business logic for pricing calculation. What is the danger of writing this logic directly inside API Mesh resolvers?
options: The client-side React UI will fail to compile
It couples logic to the gateway's deployment cycle, limits testability, and increases the difficulty of monitoring compared to an App Builder action
API Mesh will restrict the database to read-only queries
The CDN will refuse to cache the pricing queries
questions.explanation: Mesh resolvers should remain declarative (mapping, stitching). Price calculations are compute workloads that belong in version-controlled, testable serverless actions.

questions.question: When does a workload exceed App Builder's capabilities and require placement on a separate engineering platform (e.g. AWS or GCP)?
options: When the storefront is built using Next.js instead of React
When the task is long-running (>15 min), stateful, or requires heavy data volumes, persistent file systems, or specialized database drivers
Whenever the workload requires authentication with Adobe IMS
When the target deployment is located in India
questions.explanation: App Builder is serverless and stateless. Heavy compute, long-running batch jobs, or persistent disk needs belong on general cloud platforms, not short-lived FaaS functions.

questions.question: In a secure BFSI environment, how should API Mesh call a sensitive legacy SOAP service that restricts traffic by IP?
options: By writing a client-side proxy in the storefront browser code
By routing calls from API Mesh through an App Builder action acting as a gateway with static IP routing or proxying
By disabling the SOAP service's firewall
By routing all queries via public search engines
questions.explanation: API Mesh execution IPs are dynamic. Routing sensitive requests through an intermediate action allows proxying or static egress IP configuration to meet strict corporate firewall rules.

questions.question: What is the correct rollback strategy for an API Mesh configuration error deployed in production?
options: Manually delete the mesh instances inside the Developer Console
Re-apply the last known-good `mesh.json` configuration from git via `aio api-mesh:update` inside the deployment pipeline
Flush the database cache of AEM Publisher
Wait for the DNS TTL cache to expire
questions.explanation: Since API Mesh configurations are declarative, rollback is achieved by pushing the previous configuration file back through the CLI deployment process.

questions.question: How should an architect design error handling inside API Mesh when one of the secondary upstream REST APIs fails?
options: Allow the failure to bubble up and return a global HTTP 500 error to the client
Configure the Mesh schema to allow the affected fields to be nullable, enabling the client to gracefully render around missing content
Write a resolver that retries the REST API synchronously in a loop for 30 seconds
Redirect the storefront user to the login screen
questions.explanation: GraphQL architecture dictates that optional fields should be nullable so that partial upstream failures do not break the entire user experience.

questions.question: What is the security risk of deploying an App Builder action as a 'web action' without validating request signatures or IMS tokens?
options: The action's source code will be deleted from git
Any public internet user can call the action, exposing internal databases or consuming execution budgets
The action will compile slowly in the pipeline
The database will fail to execute read-write transactions
questions.explanation: Exposing actions as web-accessible makes them public. Authors must validate tokens or use HMAC signatures to verify authenticity before processing the payload.

questions.question: How is high availability and scalability managed for custom admin UIs deployed on App Builder?
options: Developers must provision additional AWS EC2 server instances in a auto-scaling group
Adobe manages the scaling and distribution automatically, as the static assets are deployed directly to Adobe's enterprise CDN
UIs must be compiled as static local files and shipped on flash drives to administrators
Scaling is limited to a maximum of 5 concurrent users
questions.explanation: App Builder UI applications are compiled to static files and served via Adobe's global CDN nodes, providing built-in scaling and high availability.

questions.question: How does an App Builder action maintain state if Adobe I/O Runtime containers are destroyed after execution?
options: By writing data to local temporary flat files on the container disk
By utilizing external databases (e.g. Postgres) or Adobe's built-in Key-Value storage service (AIO State)
Actions cannot maintain state and must be entirely stateless
By keeping variables in global memory scope
questions.explanation: Containers are ephemeral. To maintain state, actions must write to external databases or use the built-in out-of-the-box AIO State storage SDK.

questions.question: What is the recommended design pattern to avoid the 'hot-start' latency penalty in App Builder serverless actions?
options: Increase the database pool limit in AEM publisher
Minimise package size, avoid importing heavy unnecessary libraries, and use lightweight HTTP clients
Keep a background thread running in the browser console to poll the action
Deploy actions as static HTML pages
questions.explanation: FaaS containers spin up dynamically. Keeping code files small and reducing dependency bloat in `package.json` accelerates container initialization.

questions.question: Under what condition should an architect configure 'Type Merging' in API Mesh?
options: When merging CSS styles from AEM and Commerce
When two independent upstream sources return types with the same name that need to be unified into a single schema entity
When translating SOAP variables into JSON formats
When compressing database tables
questions.explanation: Type merging is used to combine fields from different APIs (e.g. AEM content and Commerce pricing) that share an identifier (like SKU) into one cohesive GraphQL type.

questions.question: How should database connection pools be handled inside an App Builder action?
options: Open a database connection on boot and keep it in a global variable indefinitely
Open the connection on request, execute queries, and close it immediately, as containers can be frozen or destroyed at any time
Bypass the database and write to flat files
Configure the database to accept infinite connections
questions.explanation: Serverless container lifetimes are untrusted. Connection leaks occur if connections are left open. Opening and closing connections per invocation prevents resource starvation.

questions.question: How should an App Builder action react to a webhook event trigger that fails with a transient error (e.g., rate limit exceeded)?
options: Fail silently and log the warning in the console
Treat event delivery as at-least-once, design action logic to be idempotent, and return a non-200 code to trigger the provider's retry loop
Spin in a CPU loop for 2 hours until the target system is up
Delete the event subscription configuration automatically
questions.explanation: Robust integrations require idempotency. Returning an error code tells the event provider (like Adobe I/O) to retry the delivery later, preventing data loss.

questions.question: What is the risk of utilizing custom, complex, inline JavaScript resolvers inside the API Mesh config?
options: The Mesh will fail to validate against JSON schema rules
Resolvers are compiled into the gateway and lack proper SRE tooling, local testing, and isolated error boundaries
It will force the storefront to use REST instead of GraphQL
The database connection pool will be locked
questions.explanation: Inline Mesh code is difficult to test, debug, and trace. Complex computation belongs in App Builder actions, keeping the Mesh configuration clean and declarative.

questions.question: How should the security contract be designed for API Mesh caching?
options: Cache all responses globally to speed up storefront load times
Verify that cached queries do not contain user-specific data or personal details, caching only public catalog or content responses
Rely on browser cookies to purge cached data on the Mesh
Encrypt cached arrays using database keys
questions.explanation: Caching personal data at the gateway level is a major data leak risk. Only public, non-personalized content (like catalog files) should be cached at the Mesh level.

questions.question: How do you verify local changes to a `mesh.json` file before updating the production gateway?
options: Deploy directly to production and test live queries
Run a local instance of the Mesh schema using the CLI or run schema validation locally against `mesh.json`
Re-compile the AEM dispatcher configuration
Local validation is not possible for API Mesh
questions.explanation: The CLI provides validation tools to run the Mesh locally or check its schema syntax against `mesh.json` before triggering a pipeline update.

questions.question: What is the recommended caching layer structure when combining API Mesh and App Builder?
options: Disable all caching to ensure real-time accuracy on every query
Cache public schemas in the Mesh gateway, cache static assets in the App Builder CDN, and use a semantic/redis cache for heavy compute actions
Store all cached data in the storefront browser's localStorage
Configure cache-control headers on the database level
questions.explanation: Layered caching optimizes performance: static CDN for assets, declarative gateway caching for catalog data, and micro-caching or caching-aside for slow computed operations.

questions.question: What does BMAD stand for in AI-native development?
options: Build-Method Automated Deployment
Brief-Method Architect-Driven Development
Business Model Architecture Document
Basic Model and Data Registry
questions.explanation: BMAD stands for Brief-Method Architect-Driven Development, a process that converts client briefs into structured contracts and architecture records before writing code.

questions.question: Where is the quality of an AI-generated codebase primarily determined in BMAD?
options: In the debugging phase
In the brief and architecture phase
In the hosting deployment phase
In the database optimization phase
questions.explanation: Under BMAD, the quality of AI output is set by the clarity of the constraints, schemas, and instructions provided in the brief phase.

questions.question: What is the role of an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) in BMAD?
options: To log the daily hours worked by developers
To capture a single significant architectural decision, its context, and its trade-offs
To auto-generate unit test files
To serve as a user documentation guide
questions.explanation: An ADR is the atomic unit of BMAD governance, recording the rationale, context, and consequences of each architecture decision.

questions.question: How many core artifacts are produced by the BMAD process?
options: One file (the prompt)
Six artifacts
Twelve databases
Fifty code repositories
questions.explanation: BMAD produces six structured artifacts: Hypothesis, Component Inventory, Contract Set, Event Registry, ADR Log, and the Stalwart Prompt Library.

questions.question: Is BMAD designed as a sequential Waterfall methodology?
options: Yes, every phase must be locked permanently before code begins
No, it is a five-phase loop producing living documents that evolve with the project
Yes, it is a sub-class of Agile Kanban
No, it only contains a single coding phase
questions.explanation: BMAD is a five-phase loop (Discovery, Architecture, Prompt Engineering, Delivery, Review) where new decisions cycle back to the architecture stage.

questions.question: What is the Hypothesis in the BMAD process?
options: A temporary guess about server scaling limits
A single paragraph stating technical approach, target outcome, and measurable success metrics check-in on day one
The client's budget estimation worksheet
An AI-generated code template
questions.explanation: The Hypothesis frames project scope, detailing what must be achieved, how, and how success is measured without ambiguity.

questions.question: What is the Component Inventory in BMAD?
options: A Jira ticket list for task allocation
An architectural register table mapping each component to its type, contract ref, and Stalwart file set
A folder containing static image assets
A list of database table schemas
questions.explanation: The Component Inventory registers all components, defining their architectural constraints (e.g. file rosters) to guide the generator prompts.

questions.question: What is a 'Contract Set' in the BMAD framework?
options: The legal contract signed with the client
Structured specifications defining file rosters, state shapes, and 'not for' logic boundaries for components
A set of environment variables for local testing
A listing of developer usernames
questions.explanation: The Contract Set defines code boundaries (e.g. prohibiting DOM updates inside business logic files) to enforce structure on the AI builder.

questions.question: What does the Event Registry define in BMAD?
options: The list of user click events tracked by analytics
The dynamic contract governing domain event names, payloads, producers, and subscribers
The calendar schedule of release deployments
A list of server system errors
questions.explanation: The Event Registry defines the communication interface between components (using names like `domain:entity:action`), ensuring messaging is decoupled and structured.

questions.question: What is the Stalwart Prompt Library?
options: A set of sample code templates stored on GitHub
A collection of structured prompt files embedding contracts, rules, and APIs used to guide the AI in generating compliant code
A library of AEM client bundles
A list of search engine optimized keywords
questions.explanation: The Stalwart Prompt Library translates architectural decisions into machine-legible instructions, embedding contracts so the AI cannot deviate.

questions.question: Why does BMAD forbid deleting old ADRs?
options: Because database storage limits prevent deletes
To preserve audit history; reversed decisions are marked 'Superseded' and link to a new ADR
Because git does not support file deletion
To ensure the codebase compiles in Cloud Manager
questions.explanation: ADRs represent history. Deleting records destroys context; instead, old decisions are marked 'Superseded' with references to the replacing ADR.

questions.question: What are the four mandatory fields in a BMAD Architecture Decision Record (ADR)?
options: Name, Date, Budget, Scope
Context, Decision, Consequences, Status
ID, File, Repository, Line
Brief, Input, Output, Review
questions.explanation: A complete ADR must contain the Context (the forces), the Decision (the choice), the Consequences (the costs/benefits), and the Status (the lifecycle state).

questions.question: What is the consequence field of an ADR designed to capture?
options: The names of developers who failed to follow instructions
The trade-offs and costs accepted, ensuring negative consequences are considered
The calendar schedule of the release pipeline
The final financial cost of the cloud resources
questions.explanation: Every decision has a cost. An ADR without stated negative consequences or trade-offs is considered incomplete and poorly analysed.

questions.question: When must an ADR be written under the BMAD methodology?
options: Three months after the system has gone live
Before the implementing pull request (PR) is merged
Only when the client requests a compliance report
At the end of each developer sprint during retrospectives
questions.explanation: ADRs must be written *before* implementation to guide code reviews. Retrospective ADR writing is archaeology, not governance.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the BMAD Hypothesis and the ADR Log?
options: They are completely independent and stored in different repositories
The Hypothesis constrains the ADR Log; decisions that do not support the Hypothesis are out of scope
The ADR Log generates the Hypothesis automatically
The Hypothesis is only written if the ADR Log is empty
questions.explanation: The Hypothesis is the project's north star. Decisions recorded in the ADR log must align with and support the outcomes stated in the Hypothesis.

questions.question: What is a 'Stalwart file set' defined in a Component Inventory?
options: A list of database backup files
The specific array of files (e.g. init, model, biz, api, render) allocated to a component
A collection of stylesheets used for responsive web design
The security keys used to log into Adobe Cloud
questions.explanation: The file set specifies the structural breakdown of the component under Stalwart design rules, constraining the AI builder from creating monolithic files.

questions.question: Which phase of the BMAD loop focuses on validating whether the generated code complies with the contract?
options: Discovery
Architecture
Review
Delivery
questions.explanation: The Review phase validates the generated code against the contract set, ensuring the pull request is compliant before merge.

questions.question: How does BMAD represent the flow of data within a component contract?
options: By writing free-form comments inside files
By declaring the 'stateShape' and enforcing boundaries (like prohibiting DOM writes in biz.js)
By letting the AI define variables dynamically at runtime
By compiling code to native database schemas
questions.explanation: Contracts govern components by defining state schemas and 'not for' constraints (e.g. render.js must not call fetch; api.js must not update UI).

questions.question: In the BMAD worked product-filter example, what is the role of the URL query string?
options: It acts as a temporary cache for image assets
It serves as the persistence layer for the filter state, ensuring bookmark-ability and shareability
It manages user session access tokens
It routes database queries to the backup nodes
questions.explanation: Per ADR-004, state.js is the runtime source of truth, but mutations are serialized to the URL query string to meet the bookmarking success criteria.

questions.question: Why does BMAD include a 'notFor' block inside its contract files?
options: To list features that the client did not pay for
To define explicit code restrictions, preventing the AI from combining logic and UI code
To specify developers who are blocked from writing code
To blacklist external API endpoints
questions.explanation: The 'notFor' configuration enforces strict separation of concerns (e.g. biz.js: direct DOM writes), preventing the AI from creating spaghetti code.

questions.question: How is the Event Registry utilized when writing Stalwart prompts?
options: It is used to check the speed of network requests
It is embedded in the prompt to define the event names, payloads, and triggers that the code must support
It is generated by the AI after code has been compiled
It replaces the need for an ADR log
questions.explanation: The prompt incorporates the event registry entries to ensure the AI creates listeners and emitters matching the exact naming and payload schemas.

questions.question: What is the recommended naming convention for event names inside the Event Registry?
options: CamelCase strings representing functions
domain:entity:action (e.g. filter:applied)
SQL query commands in lowercase
The name of the component that handles the event
questions.explanation: DEPT® guidelines recommend the `domain:entity:action` namespace format to maintain readability and avoid collision across large event buses.

questions.question: How are changes to the Event Registry or Contract Set governed when a new component feature is requested?
options: The developer edits the codebase and updates the registry retrospectively
The architect drafts a new or updated ADR, updates the contract files, and regenerates prompt templates
The AI agent updates the registry automatically on deploy
Registry modifications are disallowed after sprint zero
questions.explanation: Modifications flow through the loop: Architecture (ADR update) -> Contract update -> Prompt update -> Delivery. Code changes follow the plan.

questions.question: In the five-phase BMAD loop, what is the input to the 'Prompt Engineering' phase?
options: Vague business descriptions
Approved contracts, ADRs, component schemas, and event registries from the Architecture phase
The compiled code binary from Delivery
The client's feedback sheet
questions.explanation: Prompt Engineering relies on precise, structured outputs from the Architecture phase to create the feature prompts.

questions.question: What is the risk of not running a 'sprint zero' workshop under the BMAD methodology?
options: Cloud Manager will reject the developer accounts
The project is highly likely to encounter mid-sprint architectural pivots or misaligned component designs
The database will run out of storage space on day one
The code will fail WCAG accessibility compliance checks
questions.explanation: Sprint zero aligns the team and locks in the initial design. Skipping it leads to ad-hoc design decisions that degrade AI generation quality.

questions.question: How are ADR files organized in the codebase repository?
options: Stored inside database blobs
As numbered markdown files in a version-controlled directory (e.g. `/docs/adr/0001-storage.md`)
Inlined as comment blocks inside frontend CSS files
Shared on external team cloud drives
questions.explanation: Storing ADRs as versioned markdown files alongside the source code ensures they remain a living part of the repository, reviewable via PRs.

questions.question: Which lifecycle state in an ADR indicates that the decision is ready for implementation?
options: Proposed
Accepted
Superseded
Draft
questions.explanation: An ADR must be in the 'Accepted' status before developers (or AI agents) are allowed to merge pull requests implementing it.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'fileRoster' array in a BMAD contract file?
options: To restrict the size of files in the repo
To define the exact files that comprise the component, enforcing the Stalwart architectural pattern
To list the authors who edited the files
To check that the codebase uses correct file formats
questions.explanation: The file roster declares the expected files (like init.js, model.js, etc.) so that the AI generates exactly these files and does not combine or omit them.

questions.question: How does BMAD manage structural drift in high-volume, AI-generated codebases?
options: By blocking developer commits using corporate firewalls
By verifying each PR's code structure against the contract files during CI validation steps
By writing new code to replace the database every week
By requiring the client to review code syntax manually
questions.explanation: Governance is enforced in CI. Structural validators check that the file layout and imports match the defined contract before allowing a merge.

questions.question: In a BMAD Hypothesis template, what is the role of the 'measurable signal'?
options: To track developer velocity in story points
To define clear, target metrics (e.g. latency under 120 ms at p95) that can be verified at launch
To verify database backup schedules
To configure DNS TTL records
questions.explanation: Measurable signals ensure that success is not subjective: they represent clear, metric-driven targets that prove the architecture's efficiency.

questions.question: If an ADR decision is reversed because it caused slow performance, what happens to the ADR document?
options: It is deleted from the git history to save space
It is marked as 'Superseded' and updated with a pointer to a new ADR explaining the reversal
Its contents are replaced with the new decision directly
It is moved to a private archive folder outside the repo
questions.explanation: To maintain historical records, the old ADR is preserved in a 'Superseded' state, and the new decision is captured in a fresh ADR.

questions.question: How does the Component Inventory guide the generation of feature prompts?
options: By supplying the pricing details for cloud services
By mapping out the file set, type, and governing contracts, ensuring the prompt contains precise boundary conditions
By setting the caching headers for the edge network
By defining the user onboarding roles
questions.explanation: The Component Inventory serves as the registry: it defines what files are required and which contracts govern them, providing structured prompt inputs.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the 'Context' field in an ADR?
options: To list the names of the development team
To describe the forces, constraints, and risks that made the decision necessary, helping future readers understand the rationale
To store environment variables
To compile code templates automatically
questions.explanation: The Context section details 'why' a decision was needed, capturing the problems, requirements, and trade-offs that led to the chosen design.

questions.question: Under BMAD, who is responsible for authoring the initial Hypothesis and ADR log?
options: The client product owner
The software architect, in collaboration with the engineering lead
The AI agent compiler
The QA validation team
questions.explanation: The architect is responsible for establishing the technical governance framework, collaborating with engineering leads to ensure accuracy.

questions.question: An architect needs to enforce state separation inside a high-volume, AI-generated component. What is the best contract constraint strategy?
options: Write a code comment inside `index.js` asking the AI not to write state
Declare a strict state schema inside the contract file, and implement a CI step verifying that files like `render.js` or `api.js` contain no state mutations
Allow the AI to define state dynamically using browser localStorage
Lock the file system to prevent any state writes at runtime
questions.explanation: Enforcing contracts requires automated checks. CI tools must parse files to ensure state mutations are restricted to the designated `state.js` file.

questions.question: Why does BMAD place 'Hypothesis' at the top of the artifact dependency chain, before ADRs and contracts?
options: To document the project's financial budget limitations
To serve as the root boundary condition; all subsequent design decisions (ADRs) must be justified by their ability to achieve the Hypothesis' outcomes
Because the compilation pipeline requires it to build first
To define user credentials for Google OAuth
questions.explanation: The Hypothesis is the contract of success. If a proposed ADR cannot be traced back to fulfilling the success conditions of the Hypothesis, it is out of scope.

questions.question: In a BMAD-governed storefront project, a developer submits a PR containing a custom IndexedDB cache logic that is not documented in the ADR log. What should the reviewer do?
options: Merge the PR and document the decision later in retro
Reject the PR because it implements a significant decision that has not yet been proposed and accepted in the ADR log
Ask the developer to explain the code verbally in stand-up
Rename the files to bypass the CI contract validation checks
questions.explanation: PRs must match decisions. If a developer introduces new patterns (like IndexedDB) without an accepted ADR, the PR is rejected until the decision is reviewed and accepted.

questions.question: How do you evaluate the quality of an ADR's 'Consequences' section?
options: By verifying that it does not contain any negative points
By ensuring it documents concrete trade-offs, detailing what becomes harder or what constraints are introduced alongside what becomes easier
By checking if it list the developer names and dates
By verifying that it compiles automatically into a PDF report
questions.explanation: A quality ADR accepts trade-offs. Stating 'no negative consequences' indicates poor analysis, as every technical choice restricts other options.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of maintaining state in the URL query string (as in the product filter worked example)?
options: It increases the server's cache invalidation frequency
It guarantees shareability and back-button compliance, but query length limits must be guarded to prevent HTTP header size failures
It requires the storefront to run on Node.js instead of Java
It bypasses accessibility WCAG guidelines
questions.explanation: URL state is excellent for UX, but long strings can fail proxy limits, requiring the architect to configure a maximum parameter guard in `config.js`.

questions.question: How should an architect construct prompt instructions to ensure the AI does not write business logic inside rendering files?
options: Rely on the AI's default general coding style settings
Embed the contract's 'notFor' rules (e.g. 'render.js must not contain business rules or fetch calls') as explicit constraints in the Stalwart prompt
Deploy the code and run a script to refactor the files later
Remove render.js from the component's file roster entirely
questions.explanation: AI models follow boundaries. Explicitly declaring negative constraints ('do not write X in Y') in the prompt is highly effective at preventing structure violations.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of using free-form, unversioned prompts for code generation across a distributed team?
options: Increased licensing costs on OpenAI or Anthropic platforms
Inconsistent codebase patterns, structural drift, and varying implementation quality that breaks testing and integration
The local virtualenv failing to load Python packages
AEM Cloud Manager rejecting the deployed packages
questions.explanation: Without a governed prompt library, engineers write prompts differently, leading to massive inconsistencies and structural drift that defeats standard code reviews.

questions.question: How does the Event Registry maintain dynamic interface contracts in micro-frontend architectures?
options: By compiling code to static HTML assets
By acting as the schema definition for all cross-component messages, preventing teams from changing event payloads without changing the registry
By setting the server's database connection pool parameters
By routing all queries via public gateways
questions.explanation: The Event Registry is the API contract for events. Changes to payload shapes must update the registry first, preventing integration breakages at runtime.

questions.question: In the context of BMAD, how does a 'Stalwart prompt' differ from a generic code generation prompt?
options: It is written in Python instead of plain English
It forces the AI to output exactly the file structure, event vocabulary, and architectural constraints defined by the component's contract
It does not require any correct index mappings
It is compiled in Cloud Manager before execution
questions.explanation: Stalwart prompts are constrained inputs containing exact schemas, file rosters, and 'not for' parameters, leaving no room for AI assumptions.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of versioning ADR files inside the source code repository instead of on an external wiki?
options: It reduces the financial cost of hosting the documentation
It binds the architectural decisions to the branch state, allowing developers to see the exact rules in place at any point in the commit history
It allows the database to read records faster
It prevents the QA team from finding the files
questions.explanation: Versioning decisions inside the repo ensures they live alongside the code. A change in design is reviewable via standard git pull request flows.

questions.question: How should an architect handle a conflict between two accepted ADRs?
options: Ignore the conflict and let the development team decide at implementation
Draft a new ADR that resolves the conflict, superseding the contradictory parts of the previous records
Delete both ADRs from the repository history
Ask the AI agent to pick the correct path during compilation
questions.explanation: Conflicts must be resolved at the governance level. A new ADR must explicitly reconcile the contradictions and supersede the outdated decisions.

questions.question: How does BMAD address the risk of AI agents writing redundant helper libraries?
options: By restricting the AI's execution budget to 60 seconds
By defining a shared library schema inside the contract set, forcing the AI to import existing helpers rather than generating new ones
By manually deleting files from the workspace during review
By blocking external internet access inside the build pipeline
questions.explanation: Enforcing a shared library roster in the prompt constraints tells the AI which utility files are available, preventing the creation of duplicate helper functions.

questions.question: What is the architectural cost of introducing a new, unvetted event name into the runtime event bus?
options: It will cause Cloud Manager deployments to time out
It creates silent integration debt, as subscribers have no schema contract and the event cannot be audited via the registry
It locks the database connection pool in production
It forces the storefront to reload on every event emission
questions.explanation: Unregistered events break tracing and decoupling. They bypass schemas, introducing silent integration errors that escape compile-time validation.

questions.question: In the delivery phase of the BMAD loop, what is the best practice for tracking which AI model was used to generate a specific feature?
options: Add the AI model reference and generation parameters in the prompt log or as header comments in the generated files
Ask the developer to remember which model they used
There is no value in tracking the model reference
Store the model name in the database table schema
questions.explanation: Recording the generation model and version in file comments or prompt logs provides valuable metadata for tracing defects or quality variations in the future.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of utilizing an strict component inventory contract compared to free-form development?
options: It decreases security compliance at launch
It requires significant upstream design effort but dramatically reduces debugging, integration, and refactoring costs
It forces all code to run slower in the browser
It increases database transaction lock times
questions.explanation: BMAD requires upfront effort to define contracts, but this investment pays off by eliminating structure drift and incorrect assumptions in AI-generated code.

questions.question: How does the architect verify that the 'measurable signal' of a Hypothesis is realistic before coding starts?
options: By setting arbitrary performance targets
By running lightweight prototypes, reviewing baseline benchmarks, and verifying that the target matches customer SRE metrics
By copying success targets from unrelated projects
By letting the AI compile a random performance profile
questions.explanation: Success targets must be defensible. Architects analyze benchmarks and constraints to set achievable, quantifiable performance objectives.

questions.question: What is the core philosophy of AI Governance at DEPT®?
options: AI does all the work, and humans only manage billing
AI generates, and humans govern
AI is restricted from writing any code at all
AI manages deployment, and humans write all tests
questions.explanation: The core split is: AI generates the code blocks (components, boilerplate), but humans retain complete accountability and govern the architecture, security, and gates.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three domains that only humans should own?
options: What gets built (product intent, why)
The shape it must take (contracts, event vocabulary)
Writing the boilerplate component code and data mappings
What it means to be safe (security, compliance, audit)
questions.explanation: Writing boilerplate code can be automated and handled by AI. The three domains reserved for humans are: what is built, the shape it takes, and safety compliance.

questions.question: Why is AI Governance described as an architecture rather than a policy document?
options: Because policy documents are too expensive to write
Because governance rules must be implemented as automated checks in CI/CD, not just written in text files
Because architects are the only people who read it
Because it only applies to cloud database structures
questions.explanation: A policy that isn't enforced in CI is just intent. True governance requires automated tooling to validate contracts and layouts before merge.

questions.question: Who owns final deployment permissions in an AI-assisted engineering team?
options: The code-generation AI agent
A named human operator (developer or architect)
The client's automated webhook
The database server administrator only
questions.explanation: Human ownership of deployment is non-negotiable. An AI agent must not have autonomous permissions to release code to production.

questions.question: What is 'governance debt' in the context of AI-generated codebases?
options: The financial cost of running AI model APIs
The accumulation of un-reviewed, automated code merges that weaken the team's control and understanding of the codebase
The number of lines of documentation missing from git
A delay in database backups
questions.explanation: Governance debt accumulates rapidly when teams merge AI-generated PRs without validation, leaving the architecture unmonitored.

questions.question: Which component of the system is responsible for verifying that AI-generated code matches the defined architecture schema?
options: The storefront browser page
The Continuous Integration (CI) pipeline
The third-party payment gateway
The database indexing daemon
questions.explanation: The CI pipeline executes the automated contract and structural checks to gate non-compliant code from merging.

questions.question: In AI-native development, what does the 'AI generates' column include?
options: Security models and access policies
Components, helper functions, unit tests, and data mappings
The release strategy and deployment commands
Final product intent and business roadmap
questions.explanation: AI handles execution tasks like writing component code, tests, and API clients, leaving interface designs and safety rules to humans.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of a 'policy-only' governance framework?
options: It increases the build duration in Cloud Manager
It exists as intent but is bypassed under deadline pressure due to a lack of automated enforcement in CI
It requires the client to pay additional licensing fees
It blocks developers from using Node.js
questions.explanation: A policy-only framework has no teeth: without automated gates, team members bypass instructions to meet schedules.

questions.question: Under DEPT® AI Governance, who is accountable if an AI-generated bug takes a production system offline?
options: The AI platform provider (e.g. OpenAI or Anthropic)
The human engineering lead and architect who approved and deployed the code
The client product owner who submitted the brief
The CI pipeline compiler
questions.explanation: Accountability is human. Humans govern the release, so the final responsibility for codebase reliability rests on the team's leads.

questions.question: What is the recommended practice for attributing AI-generated code inside a git repository?
options: Use the name of the AI model as the git commit author
Annotate generated files with metadata comments and link the PR to the governing ADR
Delete the git history to anonymise authors
Commit all code under a single shared administrator account
questions.explanation: Governance requires legibility. Linking commits and files to their prompting ADRs and tracking model references supports code auditing.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a human-owned domain in AI Governance?
options: Writing standard unit tests
Security models and compliance structures
Mapping API variables
Generating boilerplate schemas
questions.explanation: Defining the security model (access control, encryption, compliance rules) is an irreducible human responsibility.

questions.question: What does a clear 'AI-vs-human split' document outline?
options: The server IP routing configuration
The explicit responsibilities of what AI can generate and what human engineers must control and review
The billing rates of creative designers
The list of third-party API keys
questions.explanation: A role-split document establishes team expectations, separating the automated tasks from the governed controls.

questions.question: What is a 'governed prompt template'?
options: A prompt that is generated dynamically by search engines
A version-controlled prompt structure approved by the architect to ensure consistent code generation quality
A text file stored on the developer's desktop
A system that bypasses CI validations
questions.explanation: Governed templates standardise prompting across the team, ensuring architectural rules and contracts are consistently injected.

questions.question: Why should changes to prompt templates require human approval?
options: To reduce compiler CPU usage
Because prompt modifications directly alter how the AI generates code, changing codebase consistency and logic boundaries
To lower API connection fees
Because git does not allow automated updates
questions.explanation: Prompts are code instructions. A change in a template can introduce structural bugs across many components, requiring review.

questions.question: In AI Governance, what is the role of an escalation and rollback path?
options: To restore the database when query connections fail
To quickly revert code changes and handle incidents when AI-generated implementations fail in production
To auto-correct code bugs at runtime in the browser
To reset developer logins on Google SSO
questions.explanation: A rollback path is a safety net: it ensures that when automated code fails, the team can immediately revert to the last green build.

questions.question: How does the architect establish a quality ceiling in an AI-augmented team?
options: By writing all the code themselves
By governing the three core domains: what is built, its shape (contracts), and safety compliance
By blocking the team from using generative AI tools
By increasing the number of QA test engineers
questions.explanation: Architects set the quality ceiling by focusing their governance on interfaces, rules, and safety, allowing AI to handle the volume safely.

questions.question: Why is the event vocabulary considered a human-owned asset in AI Governance?
options: Because events cannot be parsed by AI models
To maintain a clear and governed communication interface between systems, avoiding messy or conflicting event names
To optimize CDN static asset delivery
To minimize the code size in git
questions.explanation: The event vocabulary is the system contract. Humans govern the vocabulary to ensure decoupled components communicate cleanly and consistently.

questions.question: How is AI Governance enforced within a pull request (PR) review process?
options: By manually checking code files on a local desktop
By utilizing automated linters to check structure against contracts, requiring a senior engineer's sign-off on the linked ADR
By running SQL queries against the user database
By executing the application in debug mode only
questions.explanation: PR reviews combine automation (contract linter validation) and human oversight (verifying that the code follows the accepted ADR).

questions.question: Why must changes to the event vocabulary and contract schemas require approval from the architect?
options: To prevent changes from affecting the CDN cache headers
Because modifications can break data contracts and runtime messaging across independent components
To reduce database replication lag
To ensure the files are compressed to WebP formats
questions.explanation: Interface schemas are the glue. If changed without architectural oversight, dependent components will fail to communicate at runtime.

questions.question: How does automated contract validation in the CI pipeline protect against 'structural drift'?
options: By encrypting the source code files in git
By parsing the layout of files and imports to ensure they strictly follow the Stalwart architecture definitions
By backing up the codebase daily to a secondary repository
By blocking developer commits that are over 400 lines of code
questions.explanation: A contract checker parses PR files to confirm that directories, file names, and dependency directions adhere to the component contracts.

questions.question: In an AI-augmented team, what is the risk of having a single human reviewer approve 50 complex generated PRs a day?
options: It will cause Cloud Manager to lock the environment
Reviewer fatigue leading to rubber-stamping, which lets structural drift and bugs pass into production
The client will be billed for excessive server capacity
The git repository will grow too large
questions.explanation: Fatigue causes reviewers to overlook violations. Architects mitigate this by using automated CI contract checks to filter out flawed code.

questions.question: Under the DEPT® AI Governance model, how should security scanning (SAST/SCA) be integrated?
options: Executed manually by developers before major milestones
Run automatically in CI on every PR to scan AI-generated code for vulnerabilities and unvetted licenses
Outsourced to an external auditing team after deployment
Configured to run inside client-side browser sessions
questions.explanation: AI-generated code is prone to repeating common security flaws or importing vulnerable dependencies. Scanning must be automated in CI.

questions.question: What does a 'governance review gate' ensure before a branch can be promoted in Cloud Manager?
options: That the storefront loads in under 1 second
That all automated checks (lint, test, contract, security) have passed and a designated human has approved the release
That the database has been replicated to all nodes
That the client has signed off on the project budget
questions.explanation: Gates merge validation and authorization: checking that all static and dynamic tests are green, and that a responsible human authorises promotion.

questions.question: Why is it important to audit the prompt templates used by code generation agents?
options: To check if they contain syntax errors that fail JSON schemas
To confirm they do not contain security leaks (e.g. system keys) and that they enforce compliance guidelines
To measure the speed of model response times
To verify that they are written in Python
questions.explanation: Prompts carry instructions and context. Auditing ensures they carry the correct security rules, contracts, and do not leak internal credentials.

questions.question: How does the role of a QA developer shift under an AI Governance model?
options: They write the baseline component code instead of the AI
They focus on testing edge cases, boundary failures, and building automated validation suites, rather than basic manual path checking
They manage the budget of the AI generation tools
QA is eliminated entirely from the team
questions.explanation: Since basic code is generated, QA shifts focus to high-value testing: validating boundary conditions, security, and integration failures.

questions.question: In an AI Governance framework, how is the 'attribution ledger' implemented in practice?
options: As a local Excel sheet on the PM's computer
Via standard git commit history, using custom tags or linking commits directly to prompt IDs and ADRs
By compiling code inside the AEM Publisher console
Attribution is not tracked in git
questions.explanation: Git commits act as the ledger: linking commits directly to their prompting instructions and ADRs makes code generation legible.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of allowing AI agents to choose their own third-party dependency libraries?
options: Slow local compilation speeds
Introducing licensing violations (e.g. GPL instead of MIT) or vulnerable/malicious packages into the codebase
Increased cloud server storage size
The database refusing connection requests
questions.explanation: AI agents lack legal and safety context: they will import packages that resolve compile tasks but violate security or compliance rules.

questions.question: Under what condition should a human engineer overwrite AI-generated code manually?
options: Whenever they want to change the variable names to match personal preferences
When the AI is unable to resolve an edge case or performance bug, documenting the exception and updating the contract
Manually editing code is always forbidden
To speed up the local git commit process
questions.explanation: Engineers bypass the AI loop only for complex edge cases, documenting the deviation to keep the architecture records accurate.

questions.question: What is the role of an 'Architectural Rule Linter' in AI Governance?
options: To verify spelling inside documentation files
To validate that file paths, import structures, and dependencies do not violate the defined component boundary lines
To check that the server is responding to HTTP queries
To monitor database query speeds
questions.explanation: Linters check that the files are structured correctly (e.g. confirming that render.js does not import API utilities), preventing boundary breaks.

questions.question: Why is 'Governance Debt' more dangerous than standard technical debt?
options: It requires rewriting the database schema
It accumulates rapidly with high-volume AI code, leading to an unmanageable codebase that no human understands or controls
It prevents the storefront from caching static files
It increases billing fees for Google Cloud accounts
questions.explanation: Because AI can generate code in seconds, a team without governance will merge massive volumes of un-reviewed code, losing complete control.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach to handle AI-generated test suites?
options: Trust the tests completely and skip human review
Validate that the AI-generated tests cover real boundary cases and execute successfully, using mutation testing to verify test quality
Discard the tests and write them all by hand
Run the tests only in the local developer workspace
questions.explanation: AI-generated tests can contain logical flaws or mock away real behaviors. Human review and quality metrics (like mutation score) are essential.

questions.question: Under Indian DPDP regulations, how does AI Governance impact data residency decisions?
options: AI models decide where to host the database tables at runtime
The architect defines strict residency boundaries in the architecture, and CI gates block any code configuration routing data outside India
Data residency is handled automatically by the CDN
Data residency rules are ignored for AI-native codebases
questions.explanation: Compliance is a human responsibility. The architect configures constraints, and validation checks ensure data routing rules are preserved.

questions.question: What does a 'security audit trail' verify in an AI-governed development workflow?
options: The number of commits made per developer
That every line of merged code was either human-authored or generated via an approved prompt and scanned for vulnerabilities
The database execution speed under heavy load
The list of user search keywords
questions.explanation: An audit trail confirms that all deployed code is trace-compliant, showing that generation followed approved templates and passed security scans.

questions.question: What is the role of human SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) teams under AI Governance?
options: To write boilerplate HTML layouts
To monitor system behavior, define SLOs/SLA alerts, and manage the safety overrides and rollout gates
To review code spelling manually
To update user roles inside the login templates
questions.explanation: SRE focuses on stability: governing operational parameters, monitoring SLIs, and maintaining safety overrides during outages.

questions.question: An AI agent proposes an architectural change that optimizes database queries but violates the modular encapsulation defined in ADR-002. What is the correct response?
options: Accept the changes because database performance is the highest priority
Reject the PR because AI must not autonomously alter architectural boundaries; changes require a new proposed ADR and human review
Allow the AI to merge the changes directly to save schedule time
Configure the linter to ignore Modular Encapsulation errors
questions.explanation: Boundary rules are non-negotiable for AI. Bypassing design rules to secure local optimization introduces severe structural debt that breaks maintainability.

questions.question: What is the primary danger of giving an AI agent write permissions directly to the production database or deployment triggers?
options: It will consume additional cloud CPU resources
It creates a severe security and SRE risk, as an agent acting on a misaligned brief or malformed input can cause catastrophic outages or data leaks
The storefront will load slowly in older browsers
The development team will stop using git
questions.explanation: Deployment requires judgement and accountability. AI agents lack contextual awareness and legal responsibility, making autonomous write access highly dangerous.

questions.question: In a regulated BFSI project using AI-generated components, how does the architect verify that the code conforms to data security policies?
options: Rely on the AI model's safety settings
Implement static security analysis (SAST) and custom regex compliance checks in the CI pipeline to scan generated payloads for PII leaks
Run manual browser testing after release
Encrypt the git repository with database keys
questions.explanation: Compliance requires automated scans. Custom checks must scan generated code for PII patterns, insecure storage models, or unencrypted endpoints in the pipeline.

questions.question: How should a development team handle a situation where an AI model update (e.g. from Sonnet 4.6 to 4.7) begins generating code that fails contract checks?
options: Disable the contract checks in CI to allow deployments to continue
Freeze the model version in prompt configurations, debug the prompt templates to accommodate the model's new behavior, and validate in staging
Ask developers to rewrite the generated code manually in production
Revert the entire codebase to the initial commit
questions.explanation: Model updates change behaviors. Teams must pin model versions, test prompt template compatibility in isolation, and verify outputs before updates.

questions.question: What is the role of an 'Architectural Override' in AI Governance?
options: A database command used to reset passwords
A documented process where a senior engineer manually signs off on a contract deviation, capturing the rationale in the ADR log
An automated AI script that bypasses security checks
A stylesheet that overrides layout branding rules
questions.explanation: Overrides are exceptions. They must be explicitly signed off by a human architect and recorded in the ADR history to prevent silent, ungoverned drift.

questions.question: How do you mitigate 'hallucinated dependency' risks in AI-generated deployments?
options: Increase the timeout limit of the API calls
Configure the package registry to validate imports against a curated allowlist, and block PRs that attempt to fetch unvetted libraries
Rely on the AI to correct its own imports at runtime
Commit all package files to git binary storage
questions.explanation: Hallucinated packages are security threats. Restricting dependencies to a curated allowlist prevents AI from importing malicious or non-existent packages.

questions.question: In the context of AI Governance, what is a 'prompt injection attack' in code generation?
options: A system error that causes the AI to stop responding
Malicious input embedded in briefs or database records designed to trick the generation model into writing backdoor scripts or exposing keys
A method of injecting SQL commands into the storefront search bar
An AEM dispatcher caching failure
questions.explanation: Injection targets the generator. If the AI consumes untrusted input during prompt assembly, it can be manipulated into writing insecure code.

questions.question: What is the primary trade-off of introducing automated contract linters inside the CI/CD pipeline?
options: It increases the CPU consumption of storefront databases
It slows down build validation times slightly but guarantees architectural consistency and prevents ungoverned structural drift
It limits developers to only write code in plain text formats
It disables Google OAuth authentication routes
questions.explanation: Validation adds slight pipeline latency, but this trade-off is necessary to prevent high-volume, automated code from degrading structural integrity.

questions.question: How should an architect design the boundary line for AI-generated tests?
options: AI generates the test execution code, but humans define the success assertions and contract parameters
AI writes the assertions and humans write the mock data structures
AI is prohibited from writing test code of any kind
Tests are generated dynamically at runtime in the browser
questions.explanation: Assertions must be human-defined. If the AI generates both the code and the assertions in isolation, it will assert the correctness of its own bugs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of run-time validation in an AI Governance model?
options: To monitor developer session logout times
To catch payload schema violations or illegal data mutations at runtime that escaped static compile-time gates
To automate code generation inside the browser
To check that the CDN is active
questions.explanation: Runtime checks (such as schema validation on API borders) act as defense-in-depth, catching mismatches that slipped through static analysis.

questions.question: How do you prevent 'complacency bias' where developers assume AI-generated code is always secure?
options: By locking their local git commits
By enforcing mandatory peer reviews, running mutation testing, and rotating code ownership across features
By banning the use of AI tools on corporate machines
By sending weekly security reports to developers
questions.explanation: Enforcing code ownership, peer reviews, and using mutation metrics ensures developers inspect and verify generated code as if they wrote it.

questions.question: In a team utilizing agentic code generators, how is the 'Code Ownership' principle maintained?
options: The AI agent owns the codebase under copyright rules
A human engineer takes explicit ownership of each component, remaining responsible for its maintenance and defect resolution
Ownership is shared equally among all client stakeholders
Ownership is transferred to the cloud hosting provider
questions.explanation: Accountability requires owners. A human engineer must own each component, ensuring defects are resolved by a person with context.

questions.question: How do you secure secrets and sensitive API endpoints inside prompts sent to public cloud AI models?
options: Encrypt the secrets before adding them to the prompt text
Use placeholders in prompt templates and inject the actual credentials only at execution runtime inside the secure corporate cloud perimeter
Public AI models do not require credentials security
Hardcode credentials inside the prompt text to speed up response times
questions.explanation: Public model calls are security risks. Prompts must use placeholders, keeping actual keys inside the local runtime variables.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach to handle legacy code modifications in an AI-governed team?
options: Let the AI rewrite the entire system from scratch on every change request
Define the modifications as a delta contract, and use the prompt to instruct the AI to alter only the scoped boundaries, verifying changes in git diff
Bypass the AI and write all legacy updates manually
Re-host the legacy code on external public servers
questions.explanation: AI updates to legacy code must be bounded. Specifying change zones and verifying the git diff keeps the rest of the legacy system stable.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of running mutation testing on AI-generated test files?
options: It breaks database transaction integrity
It requires significant test suite execution time but guarantees that the tests are actually checking logic and not just passing blindly
It limits testing to only verify client-side CSS rules
It requires developers to rewrite all tests in Node.js
questions.explanation: Mutation testing is compute-heavy, but it is the only way to verify that tests are high-quality and fail when logic is altered.

questions.question: How should an architect evaluate the efficiency of the AI Governance model?
options: By tracking the total number of lines of generated code
By monitoring defect density, build failure rate, PR lead times, and the frequency of ungoverned codebase overrides
By calculating the financial budget saved on creative designers
By checking developer logins on Google SSO portals
questions.explanation: Efficiency metrics look at quality and velocity: low defect density and rapid PR lead times prove that governance balances safety and speed.
